author: @muntee33
text:
It's not just about V.  
it's much more about Htz....

Edit:
And molecular bonds.
*Remembering that the mass-energy equivalence equation can be reinterpreted using the preceding frequency-energy equivalence equation. Couple that with a sound understanding of the relevant atomic/molecular harmonic scales for the application. (just like a microwave uses such understanding to select its materials, cavity configuration, electrodynamics and HARMONICS required to target the resonant frequency of a water molecule (which is relatively simple, being a simple dipole molecule) to induce an electric charge to excite the molecule. 
"But that's not the same" you say?
Horse for courses...
All I'm saying is the public understanding of the current electrolysis method is a very crude and inefficient method. In the vicinity of <5% of what we could be easily capable of with current materials and tooling. 10-20yrs of R&D.... That number is an order of magnitude greater than what we could/would be capable of.

author: @muntee33
text:
EXACTLY why the automotive industry is promoting EV's while actively discrediting Hydrogen as a viable fuel source. While the mining/frieght/shipping/aviation/heavy machinery sectors (EACH of which DWARF the public automotive markets hydrocarbon fuel requirements..... ) are all universally moving towards developing a hydrogen ICE market and/or hydrogen fuel cell market. 

One might be a critical sceptic and perceive this as not an interest based on profitability and efficiency/proficiency but rather a move to further the control over the publics dependancy on controlled and centralised markets.... Imagine 20 yrs after EVs are 99% of registered vehicles, all the tooling and knowledge of ICE will be long gone and with both the family household and transport powered by the same source the ability to escape market pressure/influence by using alternate sources to power either fuel/energy requirement will not be a viable alternative most the vast majority. Meaning the market controllers will have a better metric to understand the acceptance and/or effectiveness of ever more profitable or incentivised business strategies, both of which are increasingly adverse to the individual and public interest. 
Just think how useful some components of obsolete technology are and the increasing difficulty of acquiring them. Ie; imagine trying to do this once microwaves are obsolete. *Components from old-school neon lighting are highly valued by enthusiasts for thier properties, as are the large Fresnel lens from old flat screen TV's (of which I'm unaware of being available anywhere else and only credit card sized units for emergency kits being easily located. Very weak A4 sized units can be found but are pretty useless. 
Same goes for cathode ray TV's this seemingly forgotten staple of the household us one of the most valuable instruments one can have for conducting conceptual proofs with magnetism, 'experiments' which challenge the entire paradigm of magnetism as taught in the educational system. 

The other topic of interest relating to my original premise is the science behind the electrolysis process we have all been taught at school from early childhood, and the same process used to discredit the technology for extracting hydrogen form water. This is school yard level science....

Surely not only the metallurgy and chemical composition of the fluid/catylist play a part, but how about the properties of the electricity? Coupled with the nature of its application, and the tuning parameters of the  cavity to harness the power of resonance to increase the efficiency of the process? How about the pressure inside the cavity? How about adding exotic metals/alloys with specific electric/magnetic properties into the equation? Along with the geometry of thier arrangement to make further use of nature? 
So much development to be done, in respect our 'modern' understanding of electrolysis is like still having cars that were designed and developed in the very early 1900's. 
I think it's a deliberate retardation of the collective understanding of such tech to limit the population from giving the energy cartel the bird and making thier own energy....


Has anyone here seen the inner workings of a microwave? (Rhetorical) this is exactly what I'm talking about with regards to more effectively creating hydrogen from water. Just look at the intuitive design of the microwave, along with the very specific materials required for it to function. 
The tuning of the cavity...
The reconfiguration of the electricity...
The use of resonance in the cavity....

Apply this understanding to electroysis and we are looking at a different paradigm. Of course, it would somehow have to be of benefit to the captain's of the energy cartel, but if it were.... I'd place a big bet that we'd be hearing and seeing a completely different narrative...
Eg, Elon Musk would have a completely different script to learn and perform in the public media. Hydrogen fuel cars would not be being prohibited from being imported for public sale, (or not sale at all in Australia actually, as our government doesn't trust it's people not to pull one apart to see how it works. For some wierd reason. They are insanely expensive even just to lease for a yr or so, the equivalent of buying a European luxury saloon outright. And only approved people/bodies/entities can even lease one, pretty much only government departments....  Yet the Prime Minister is quoting in the media that Australia is going to be the world leader in hydrogen production and exportation... Go figure)

author: @grazianoturbogas

Hallo, after having done a ton of experiments, i still do not get the point of electrolyzing urea.

That's AdBlue with 5% on NaOH added.
The resoult is a dramatic loss of efficiency compared with NaOH solution in water by itself.
What can I change to my process? Thanks.

author: @Atimar01
text:
you tested straight electrolisis with h2so4 yet?
voltage drops to 1.25 V or so ....
so also efficient and you only actually use water.
automatic filler on it to level keep the water at the around same level and there you are.
just add h2so4 until the current you draw is around  0.1A  per cm2 for efficiency. 
yes, only 100 mA per cm2, 50 is even better.
more current and the voltage will rise and there goes the efficiency :o 

most ppl think h2so4 ... yikes battery acid dangeroud !!
but so ar all chemicals at high concentration 
i wouldn't wanna touch KOH at high concentration since its pretty strong.
h2so4 in something like a 1 or 2% solution is not more dangerous than KOH for the same amperage.
so basically a 10x10 cm plate gets to draw around 10A max but 5 would be prefered.

want more gass? use neutral plates or just bigger .
i'm working on a large unit for some time now,  has 240 or so plates and works on (mains seperated) straight rectified DC from the 230V mains.
should produce an insane amount of HHO.
a basic motor dimmer will be used to regulate the amount HHO.

author: @PressToDigitate
text: *ROBERT:* Thank you for finally tackling the Urea Hydrogen issue!  If you'll recall, I bugged you for months to do so, a couple years back.  Now, please take it to the next level, by doing it with net DC power OUT rather than feeding power IN -- by substituting *ALUMINUM* (as in crushed waste Beverage Cans) for your Stainless Steel Anode.  By switching to NaOH over KOH, you will find that the caustic becomes a true Catalyst, replenishing continuously during the reaction. [When the caustic is sufficiently concentrated to expedite the reaction, the polymer can coatings dissolve away in it with no problem.]  It will generate Hydrogen, DC Current and Substantial Heat Energy, as long as there is Aluminum for it to react.  The practical issue with making this commercially useful is figuring out a mechanics by which additional crushed cans can be continuously added to the system, without opening the gas evolution chamber to the atmosphere.  Capturing the Hydrogen and the DC is pretty straightforward, and a heat exchange loop (or Thermoelectric Chips) to collect the Heat energy can be easily sorted out.  But Free Hydrogen & DC Current from waste Soda & Beer cans is pretty nifty.  The Energy embodied in the cans' Aluminum is worth far more than the metal itself is, as a recyclable.  205 Watt Hours per 355ML Can.  Have at it!

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text:
Instead of crushing the cans, could you cut off the top and bottom, slit it to make a single layer sheet, then run these through a good paper shredder?  

Maybe you could incorporate such a spreading device into your design and have that be the way you add the aluminum? How often would the device need more aluminum? Is this an hourly thing? Once a week or a month?

replies.author: @PressToDigitate
replies.text: @colleenforrest7936 It depends how much power you want it to produce.  Crushing them vertically can yield a fairly uniform 'disc' shaped "puck" that is fairly compact. The flat sheets obtainable from the cylinder sides would be easy to use in themselves, but cutting the ends off reliably to do so is a challenge, and then you have the leftover ends, which are the most reactive parts, since they are uncoated with label paint.

replies.author: @herenow2895
replies.text: This sounds very interesting. Have you put it to the test or is it theoretical ?

replies.author: @PressToDigitate
replies.text: @herenow2895 Yes, I have, and the Aluminum releases a lot of energy (as many other Aluminum Battery/Fuel Cell projects have demonstrated). Using *waste* Aluminum, and Pairing it with Urine optimizes it.  I've done experiments, but am not set up to quantify the Power, Hydrogen and Heat outputs, the way that an experienced Chemist like Robert is.  To see him get his little motor running, *while* making Hydrogen off it, even just measuring the Temperature (without even trying to do anything with the thermal energy) would be dramatic.  Wherever people are emptying beverage cans, they are producing Urine, so such a system could definitely find applications.  In my testing, the Heat and Hydrogen were both impressive; but RMS would get a good handle on the Current you could extract from it as well.  Each Aluminum atom releases *Three* Electrons as it Oxidizes.

author: @masteragario3335
text:
You can not electrolyse water at 3.2v or any other voltage. Water does not conduct electricity as you very well know. Unless you mean you can electrolyse contaminated water that is exposed to a source of H and O.... air. Use distilled water and seal the water from the air. Or....Use an electrolyte that does not have O or H, seal the solution from the air. It may well elaectrolyse and you will get gases but there will be Zero O or H. It really flummox's me that you have a very enquiring mind and question everything..... other than the official narrative. Are you like most whereby self preservation trumps the truth? 

I will prove my point. Distilled water does not boil in a vacuum (as science leads us to believe), I have a vacuum chamber and have done the experiment many times. Add a bit of salt and it will appear, to boil but it doesn't boil. Given time (a few minutes), the gases are forced out of the liquid and eventually the "boiling" stops whilst thte vacuum is maintained, again I have done this many times. All the videos on YT cut short, they show the bubbles and claim boiling. job done. 
I am feeling brave, I postulate that both elctrolysis and a large reduction in pressure achieve the same thing which is to break the bonds between the water and the disolved gases forcing the gases to expand. I further postulate that a drop in pressure whilst performing electrolysis will reduce the required voltage to perform electrolysis.

replies.author: @masteragario3335
replies.text:
I just did a few more experiments with hydrolysis in my vacuum chamber. A bit crude, two 4 inch screws attatched to a usb battery pack 5v. Found an accidental cool tool, a safety feature of the battery pack is a low draw shut off and auto on when there is a draw on the battery. It instantly shows if the solution conducts electricity.
1. Distilled water does not elecrtolyse or boil at all in vacuum. (expected).
2. Distilled water with salt slowly added, almost a grain at a time until the battery kicks in and electrolysis starts. Electrodes removed, just the solution placed in vacuum. Bubbled for a few minutes then stopped. When out of the vacuum, the solution no longer triggers the battery, it is no longer drawing enough power. More salt added until battery triggered.
3. Distilled water and lots of salt and electrodes placed in vacuum. Bubbled random and aggressively at vaccum, brown yellow colouring. Colouring and bubbling continued, sludge build up in bottom of glass. bubbling reuced  in size and local to the electrodes but continued throughout. 
4. Distilled water with salt slowly added until electrolysis starts. Placed in vacuum with electrodes. Bubbled random and aggressively at vaccum, brown yellow colouring. Colouring quickly stopped, bubbling reuced  in size and local to the electrodes but continued throughout. 

I learn't many things but one thing stood ou for met. Mid way through the experiments, the rate of reaction dropped by maybe 75% but continued throughout. Although the reaction appears to continue, the affects of the reaction appears to stop ie Colouring and sludge build up. 
So whats going on?? Why is the reaction appearing to continue but no resulting after effects?? What is actually happening on a micro level is element effect at the electrode, The water touching the electrode is being rapidly heated (micro level), imagine dropplets of water in a hot frying pan. 

I postulate that once the disolved gases from the electrolyte have been expelled / spent the consequent bubbling is not electolysis and no gas is released. I plan to confirm this by collecting and testing any gases produced in the vacuum over the duration. That will take bit more jiggery pokery to sort out.

replies.author: @patomahony9747
replies.text:
@seekerscovereal worked in a facility that had a hydrogen fuelled furnace. Can’t rem if it was in cylinders or in a large tank. From what I can remember static electricity was an issue as was temperature . 
Below 800c it is in furnace applications highly explosive if injected in large quantities. Think this was a sudden rise to a concentration of around 30%. 
Reason it is used in coating furnace operations is it’s clean burning properties. 
Unlike other gasses that have additives to give them a smell in case of a leak. This is apparently not possible with hydrogen. 
All in all if handled correctly just as safe as other fuels ie lng or petroleum products just has different requirements
